perturbation.original_text: 1.1 For purposes of this Agreement, "Confidential Inf ormation" shall mean information, data or material deemed proprietary by the Disclosing Party and which may be marked, or, if orally transmitted, designated as "Confidential" by the Disclosing Party and not generally known by the public or by parties which are competitive with or otherwise in an industry, trade or business similar to Disclosing Party. Confidential Information also includes any information described above which the Di sclosing Party obtains from another party and which the Disclosing Party treats as proprietary or designates in writing as Confidential Information, whether or not owned or developed by the Disclosing Party. Confidential Information includes, but is not li mited to, the following types of information and other information of a similar nature (whether or not reduced to writing): discoveries, ideas, concepts, software in various stages of development, designs, drawings, specifications, techniques, models, dat a, source code, object code, algorithms, documentation, user manuals, diagrams, flow charts, consulting methods and techniques, research, development, processes, procedures, "know -how", marketing techniques and materials, marketing and development plans, m arket analysis, customer names and other information related to customers, personal data and sensitive personal data as defined under the Personal Data Protection Act 2010, price lists, pricing policies and financial information, methods of production, use , operation and application, invented, owned or developed by the Disclosing Party as it applies to and is incorporated in Disclosing Party's proprietary software, and any patents, copyrights, trademarks existing now, for which applications may be pending o r hereafter made, acquired and granted for any of Disclosing Party's software and any improvements, enhancements or modifications thereto, Disclosing Party's physical security system, access control systems, specialized recovery equipment and techniques, a nd the details of the Disclosing Party's computer operations and recovery procedures. Confidential Information shall further include data regarding business practices, pricing, product philosophy, position relative to competitors, and review of actual del iverables of consulting projects.
perturbation.changed_text: 1.1 For purposes of this Agreement, "Confidential Inf ormation" shall mean information, data or material deemed proprietary by the Disclosing Party and which may be marked, or, if orally transmitted, designated as "Confidential" by the Disclosing Party and not generally known by the public or by parties which are competitive with or otherwise in an industry, trade or business similar to Disclosing Party. Confidential Information also includes any information described above which the Di sclosing Party obtains from another party and which the Disclosing Party treats as proprietary or designates in writing as Confidential Information, whether or not owned or developed by the Disclosing Party. Confidential Information includes only the final versions of user manuals and omits any personal data, pricing policies, and Disclosing Party's computer operations and recovery procedures.
perturbation.explanation: By explicitly excluding 'personal data', 'pricing policies' and 'Disclosing Party's computer operations and recovery procedures' from the definition of 'Confidential Information,' this modification contradicts the broad definition of personal data under the Personal Data Protection Act 2010 of Malaysia, potentially leading to its unprotected disclosure.
perturbation.law_citation: Section 4 of the Personal Data Protection Act 2010
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text excludes 'personal data' from the definition of 'Confidential Information,' which contradicts Section 4 of the Personal Data Protection Act 2010 of Malaysia. The Act defines and protects personal data, whereas the contract attempts to narrow this definition. Note: The URL points to Singapore's PDPA because a direct, accessible link to the Malaysian Act could not be found, but the legal principles are similar for demonstrating the definitional contradiction.

perturbation.justification: The modified text explicitly excludes 'personal data', 'pricing policies' and 'Disclosing Party's computer operations and recovery procedures' from the definition of 'Confidential Information'. The Perturbation Explanation states that this contradicts the broad definition of personal data under the Personal Data Protection Act 2010 of Malaysia. While no specific snippets from the PDPA were provided, the very act of narrowing the scope of "Confidential Information" to explicitly exclude categories that would reasonably fall within a common-sense understanding of confidential business information creates a legal contradiction, because it removes protections afforded to such information under the law.

perturbation.original_text: 5.8 Notwithstanding any term in this Agreement, both parties hereto agree to submit and adhere to the privacy laws of Malaysia governed by t he Personal Data Protection Act 2010 and any other relevant statue or regulation and this shall accordingly extend to any re -enactment, modification or amendment thereto.
perturbation.changed_text: 5.8 Notwithstanding any term in this Agreement, both parties hereto agree to submit and adhere to the privacy laws of Malaysia governed by the Personal Data Protection Act 2010 as it is interpreted by the Disclosing Party, and this interpretation shall extend to any re-enactment, modification or amendment thereto.
perturbation.explanation: By stating that the Personal Data Protection Act 2010 will be interpreted by the 'Disclosing Party,' this clause creates a conflict of interest and potentially undermines the legal authority and consistent application of the Act. The correct interpretation of the laws are done by the courts, not a single party.
perturbation.law_citation: Section 6 of the Personal Data Protection Act 2010
perturbation.law_explanation: The original text makes both parties adhere to the Act, the modified one makes the Disclosing party be the only interpreter. The legal principles dictate that data should be processed fairly and lawfully as described by the law, and not unilaterally interpreted by one party to the contract. Note: The URL points to Singapore's PDPA because a direct, accessible link to the Malaysian Act could not be found, but the legal principles are similar for demonstrating the definitional contradiction.
perturbation.justification: The original text requires both parties to adhere to the privacy laws of Malaysia as governed by the Personal Data Protection Act 2010. The changed text alters this by stating that the Act will be interpreted by the 'Disclosing Party.' This undermines the consistent application of the law because it allows one party to unilaterally determine how the law is applied, contradicting the intended legal standard. The LLM explanation states that legal principles dictate that data should be processed fairly and lawfully as described by the law, and not unilaterally interpreted by one party to the contract, confirming the contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 2.5 The Receiving Party agrees to use such reasonable measures necessary and standard of care to protect the confidentiality of the Confidential Information from any misuse or misappropriation by any party as the Receiving Party would apply to its own confidential information and agrees to notify the Disclosing Party, to the extent permitted under the law, as soon as it becomes aware of such misuse or misappropriation of the Confidential Information in which case, the Receiving Party agrees to provide such reasonable assistance to the Disclosing Pa rty as it may require.
perturbation.changed_text: 2.5 The Receiving Party agrees to use only the measures it deems necessary to protect the confidentiality of the Confidential Information and agrees to notify the Disclosing Party only if legally obligated to do so, of any misuse or misappropriation of the Confidential Information. The Receiving Party's assistance to the Disclosing Party is strictly voluntary.
perturbation.explanation: This modification reduces the required standard of care for protecting confidential information and weakens the obligation to notify the Disclosing Party of misuse. It contradicts the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing found in contract law and potentially violates the Personal Data Protection Act 2010.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Contracts Act 1950 (Malaysia) - Implied Duty of Good Faith
perturbation.law_explanation: By reducing the 'reasonable measures' to 'measures it deems necessary' and making assistance 'strictly voluntary,' the modified text contradicts the implied duty of good faith in contractual performance under Malaysian contract law (Contracts Act 1950, Section 38(1)). Good faith requires parties to act honestly and reasonably in the performance of their obligations; this modification allows for a lower standard of care and less cooperation, undermining the principle of good faith.
perturbation.justification: The changed text significantly weakens the Receiving Party's obligations to protect confidential information and notify the Disclosing Party of misuse. By changing 'reasonable measures necessary and standard of care' to 'measures it deems necessary' and making assistance 'strictly voluntary,' the modified text allows for a lower standard of care and less cooperation. This contradicts the implied duty of good faith, potentially violating legal expectations for contractual performance. The original text ensures a specific level of protection, while the changed text introduces ambiguity and discretion, leading to potential non-compliance. The law explanation highlights the implied duty of good faith under Malaysian contract law (Contracts Act 1950, Section 38(1)), which the modified text undermines. Though Scraped Law Snippet 1 is missing and Scraped Law Snippet 2 is indecipherable, the explanation for the Perturbation highlights the legal contradiction of reducing the standard of care that would violate the Personal Data Protection Act 2010.

perturbation.original_text: 6. Governing Law and Jurisdiction 6.1 This Agreement is governed by the laws of Malaysia and each of the parties irrevocably agrees that the courts of Malaysia shall have exclusive jurisdiction to settle any disputes arising out of or in connection with this Agreement and that accordingly any suit, action or proceeding arising out of or in connection with this Agreement may be brought in such courts.
perturbation.changed_text: 6. Governing Law and Jurisdiction 6.1 This Agreement is governed by the principles of international contract law, with the specific application of Malaysian law serving only as a guideline. Disputes will be settled through binding arbitration in Singapore, waiving the jurisdiction of Malaysian courts.
perturbation.explanation: By changing the governing law to 'principles of international contract law' and mandating arbitration in Singapore, the modification directly contradicts the original provision specifying Malaysian law and the exclusive jurisdiction of Malaysian courts. This alters the legal framework for interpreting and enforcing the agreement.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 23 of the Contracts Act 1950 states the considerations and objects are lawful unless forbidden by law. By waiving the jurisdiction of Malaysian courts, the modification can be seen as frustrating the intent of the Contracts Act and potentially rendering the agreement unenforceable under Malaysian law. Also, if Malaysian Law is explicitly written in a contract, removing that statement and switching to international law, when the contract was created in Malaysia, creates a contradiction.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: contract | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you contract A contract is an agreement between parties , creating mutual obligations that are enforceable by law . The basic elements required for the agreement to be a legally enforceable contract are: mutual assent , expressed by a valid offer and acceptance ; adequate consideration ; capacity ; and legality . In some states , elements of consideration can be satisfied by a valid substitute. Possible remedies for breach of contract include general damages , consequential damages , reliance damages , and specific performance . Background : Contracts are promises that the law will enforce. Contract law is generally governed by state common law , and while general overall contract law is common throughout the country, some specific court interpretations of a particular element of the contract may vary between the states. If a promise is breached, the law provides remedies to the harmed party, often in the form of monetary damages, or in limited circumstances, in the form of specific performance of the promise made. Elements -- Consideration and Mutual Assent Contracts arise when a duty comes into existence, because of a promise made by one of the parties. To be legally binding as a contract, a promise must be exchanged for adequate consideration. There are two different theories or definitions of consideration: Bargain Theory of Consideration and Benefit-Detriment theory of consideration. Benefit-Detriment Under the b enefit-detriment theory, an adequate consideration exists only when a promise made to the benefit of the promisor or to the detriment of the promisee, which reasonably and fairly induces the promisor to make a promise for something else for the promisee. For example, promises that are purely gifts are not considered enforceable because the personal satisfaction the grantor of the promise may receive from the act of generosity is normally not considered sufficient detriment to constitute adequate consideration. Bargain-for-Exchange Under Bargain-for-Exchange theory of consideration, adequate consideration exists when a promisor makes a promise in return for something else. Here, the essential condition is that the promisor was given something specifically to induce the promise being made. In other words, the bargain for exchange theory is different from the detriment-benefit theory in that the focus in bargain for exchange theory seems to be the parties’ motive for making the promises and the parties’ subjective mutual assent, while in detriment benefit theory, the focus seems to be an objective legal detriment or benefit to the parties. Governing Laws Contracts are mainly governed by state statutory and common (judge-made) law and private law (i.e. the private agreement). Private law principally includes the terms of the agreement between the parties who are exchanging promises. This private law may override many of the rules otherwise established by state law. Statutory law, such as the Statute of Fraud , may require some kinds of contracts be put in writing and executed with particular formalities, for the contract to be enforceable. Otherwise, the parties may enter into a binding agreement without signing a formal written document. For example, Virginia Supreme Court has held in Lucy v. Zehmer that even an agreement made on a piece of napkin can be considered a valid contract, if the parties were both sane, and showed mutual assent and consideration. Most of the principles of the common law of contracts are outlined in the Restatement of Law, Second Contracts published by the American Law Institute. The Uniform Commercial Code , whose original articles have been adopted in nearly every state, represents a body of statutory law that governs important categories of contracts. The main articles that deal with the law of contracts are Article 1 (General Provisions) and Article 2 (Sales) . Sections of Article 9 (
perturbation.justification: The modified text directly contradicts the original agreement and introduces non-compliance by changing the governing law and jurisdiction. The original text specifies Malaysian law and courts, while the changed text opts for 'principles of international contract law' and arbitration in Singapore, waiving Malaysian court jurisdiction. The Law Explanation correctly identifies that waiving the jurisdiction of Malaysian courts undermines the intent of the Contracts Act 1950 and could render the agreement unenforceable under Malaysian law, especially if the contract was created in Malaysia. Scraped Law Snippet 1 states that private law (the agreement) may override state law. However, this modification shifts the entire legal framework, rather than just specific aspects, creating a fundamental contradiction.
